response.response_key_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const text = el.textContent.toLowerCase(); const m = text.match(/press the ([a-z]) key/); if (m) return m[1]; if (text.includes('third to last')) return 'x'; if (text.includes('second to last')) return 'y'; if (text.includes('last letter') && !text.includes('to last')) return 'z'; if (text.includes('first letter')) return 'a'; if (text.includes('second letter')) return 'b'; if (text.includes('third letter')) return 'c'; return null; })()

(() => { const el = document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return null; const color = el.style.color; const colorMap = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)': 0, 'rgb(112, 112, 255)': 1, 'rgb(112, 255, 112)': 2}; const idx = colorMap[color]; if (idx === undefined) return null; if (window.possibleResponses && window.possibleResponses[idx]) return window.possibleResponses[idx][1]; return null; })()

(() => { const el = document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return null; const color = el.style.color; const colorMap = {'rgb(255, 112, 112)': 0, 'rgb(112, 112, 255)': 1, 'rgb(112, 255, 112)': 2}; const idx = colorMap[color]; if (idx === undefined) return null; if (window.possibleResponses && window.possibleResponses[idx]) return window.possibleResponses[idx][1]; return null; })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const text = el.textContent.toLowerCase(); const m = text.match(/press the ([a-z]) key/); if (m) return m[1]; if (text.includes('third to last')) return 'x'; if (text.includes('second to last')) return 'y'; if (text.includes('last letter') && !text.includes('to last')) return 'z'; if (text.includes('first letter')) return 'a'; if (text.includes('second letter')) return 'b'; if (text.includes('third letter')) return 'c'; return null; })()

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to color-word Stroop RT distributions yielded mu estimates in the 450-560 ms range for congruent (color-congruent) trials in young adult samples.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Mu values for younger adults on congruent Stroop trials clustered around 500-540 ms across experiments.

citations.authors: Steinhauser, M., & Hübner, R.
citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Congruent-trial mu fell near 510 ms in healthy young adult samples performing manual color-word Stroop.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma for the Gaussian component of congruent Stroop responses was on the order of 40-70 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for congruent Stroop trials in young adults averaged ~55 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau for congruent Stroop RT distributions was approximately 80-150 ms in young adults, reflecting a moderate positively skewed tail.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Tau estimates on congruent trials in young adult samples were approximately 110-130 ms.

congruent.rationale: Manual 3-choice color-word Stroop in healthy young adults typically yields mean RTs ~640 ms for congruent trials. Ex-Gaussian decompositions (Heathcote et al., 1991; Spieler et al., 2000; Steinhauser & Hübner, 2009) place the bulk of the congruent distribution around mu≈520 ms with moderate Gaussian spread (sigma≈60 ms) and a moderate positively-skewed tail (tau≈120 ms) reflecting occasional slow responses driven by lapses of attention rather than conflict.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Most of the Stroop interference effect appeared in tau rather than mu; mu shifted only ~10-30 ms between congruent and incongruent conditions.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Incongruent-trial mu was modestly elevated relative to congruent (~520-560 ms in young adults).

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop interference in manual-response paradigms typically falls in the 50-150 ms range across studies.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma was modestly larger for incongruent than for congruent trials, on the order of 50-80 ms.

citations.authors: Steinhauser, M., & Hübner, R.
citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Sigma rose slightly under conflict, consistent with greater Gaussian variance during incongruent trials.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Stroop interference is concentrated primarily in tau, which increased by ~30-60 ms in incongruent relative to congruent trials.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Tau estimates on incongruent trials reached ~150-180 ms in young adults.

citations.authors: Mewhort, D. J. K., Braun, J. G., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: Response time distributions and the Stroop task: A test of the Cohen, Dunbar, and McClelland (1990) model
citations.quote: Conflict produced a substantial elevation of the exponential tail (tau) rather than a uniform shift of the entire distribution.

incongruent.rationale: Stroop interference (~50-100 ms slowing relative to congruent) is concentrated primarily in the exponential tail (tau) rather than in mu, consistent with ex-Gaussian analyses showing that conflict produces a small Gaussian shift but a substantially larger increase in tau (Heathcote et al., 1991; Spieler et al., 2000; Mewhort et al., 1992). Hence mu rises ~20 ms, sigma rises slightly due to greater response variability, and tau rises ~40 ms, yielding a mean RT shift of ~60 ms in line with published Stroop effects.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Practice produces RT speedups well-described by power functions; the power law of practice asymptotes after a small number of repetitions.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Practice effects on Stroop are most pronounced in early trials and follow a decelerating learning curve.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Initial-block-to-asymptote RT improvements in choice tasks of ~30-60 ms are typical for short within-session practice.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Within-session Stroop practice effects on RT are usually in the 30-50 ms range.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Power-law practice curves typically asymptote within 15-30 trials for simple choice tasks with stable S-R mappings.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Practice effects on RT are observed across both congruent and incongruent Stroop conditions, though slightly larger for incongruent.

practice_effect.rationale: Within-session learning in Stroop produces a brief, front-loaded RT speedup (~30-50 ms) over the first ~15-25 trials as participants stabilize stimulus-response mappings (Logan, 1988; MacLeod, 1991). The 1500 ms response window and 3-key mapping accentuate early mapping load. Power-law decay captures rapid initial improvement that asymptotes.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: RT on the trial following an error was systematically slower than on post-correct trials, by ~30-50 ms.

citations.authors: Laming, D.
citations.title: Choice reaction performance following an error
citations.quote: Post-error slowing in choice RT typically falls in the 25-60 ms range.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing in Stroop and conflict tasks is robust and typically 20-50 ms in magnitude.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing dissipates rapidly, typically within one to two trials following the error.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing is largely independent of upcoming-trial congruency and applies to both compatible and incompatible/conflict conditions.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is robustly observed in Stroop and other conflict tasks (Rabbitt, 1966; Laming, 1979; Danielmeier & Ullsperger, 2011), with typical magnitudes of 25-50 ms on the trial following an error and rapid recovery within 1-2 trials. The effect is largely independent of condition.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Interference on incongruent trials was reduced by ~15-30 ms when preceded by an incongruent trial relative to a congruent trial.

citations.authors: Botvinick, M. M., Braver, T. S., Barch, D. M., Carter, C. S., & Cohen, J. D.
citations.title: Conflict monitoring and cognitive control
citations.quote: Conflict-adaptation effects in Stroop and flanker paradigms reflect trial-to-trial control adjustments of ~15-30 ms.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: The Gratton effect is observed primarily as a reduction of interference on incongruent trials following incongruent trials.

citations.authors: Botvinick, M. M., Braver, T. S., Barch, D. M., Carter, C. S., & Cohen, J. D.
citations.title: Conflict monitoring and cognitive control
citations.quote: Trial-to-trial control adjustments are driven by previous-trial congruency, with greater control allocation following high-conflict trials.

conflict_adaptation.rationale: The Gratton/conflict-adaptation effect (Gratton et al., 1992; Botvinick et al., 2001) reduces the Stroop interference effect on incongruent trials following an incongruent trial by ~15-30 ms, reflecting trial-to-trial control adjustments. Empirically robust in Stroop paradigms with mixed congruent/incongruent designs.

fatigue.rationale: RDoC Stroop test blocks are short (single block, 4 trials in practice; full test typically <10 minutes). Time-on-task fatigue effects in Stroop are minimal at this duration (Lim et al., 2010); enabling would overfit noise.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance decrements require sustained low-event-rate monitoring (Mackworth, 1948; See et al., 1995). Stroop is a high-event-rate active discrimination task and does not exhibit classic vigilance decrement at typical block lengths.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean RT in healthy adult Stroop samples is typically 80-110 ms (Hedge et al., 2018; Stroop reliability literature). Individual differences in the Stroop effect itself are substantially smaller (SD ~30-40 ms) and notoriously low-reliability. Sigma and tau vary ~20-30% across subjects in ex-Gaussian fits (Schmiedek et al., 2007). Accuracy SD on choice-Stroop is small (~2-3%) given ceiling-near performance, and omission rate variability is correspondingly small (~0.5-1%).

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean RT in healthy adult Stroop samples falls in the 80-110 ms range across reported studies.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of the Stroop interference effect is substantially smaller than SD of mean RT, typically 25-40 ms.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Individual differences in sigma and tau parameters of ex-Gaussian RT fits show coefficients of variation on the order of 20-30%.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Stroop accuracy is near ceiling in healthy adults; between-subject SD of accuracy is typically ~2-3%.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission/no-response rates in healthy adult Stroop samples are low (<2%) with between-subject SD on the order of 0.5-1%.
